name: Tumatauenga
description: Tumatauenga, in Māori mythology, is the god of war and humanity. He is one of the children of Ranginui (sky father) and Papatūānuku (earth mother). In the beginning, Rangi and Papa were locked in a close embrace, preventing light from entering the world. Tumatauenga was the most fierce and resolute of his brothers, advocating for the separation of their parents, even if it meant violence. He proposed killing Rangi and Papa, but his brothers favored gentler methods. Ultimately, Tāne Mahuta, god of the forest, separated them. Enraged by the forced separation of his parents, Tumatauenga sought vengeance on his brothers. He captured and consumed Tāne's children (birds), Tangaroa's children (fish), Rongo's children (cultivated plants), and Haumia-tiketike's children (uncultivated plants), thus establishing humanity's dominion over these domains. This act of aggression and consumption is seen as the origin of warfare and the human condition, where humans are perpetually at war with nature and each other. Tumatauenga represents the untamed aspects of human nature, including aggression, strength, and the will to survive. He is often invoked in rituals related to war, hunting, and other activities requiring courage and strength. His actions also highlight the interconnectedness of all things and the consequences of disrupting the natural order.
subtitle: God of War and Humanity's Origin

keyMyths.title: The Separation of Rangi and Papa
keyMyths.description: Tumatauenga advocates for the violent separation of his parents, Ranginui and Papatūānuku, to bring light into the world. His actions set the stage for conflict and change.

keyMyths.title: Tumatauenga's Revenge
keyMyths.description: Angered by the separation, Tumatauenga seeks revenge on his brothers by capturing and consuming their children. This act establishes humanity's dominion over the natural world.
keyMyths.source: Orbell, The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Māori Mythology

keyMyths.title: Origin of Warfare
keyMyths.description: Tumatauenga's actions are seen as the origin of warfare and the human condition of perpetual conflict. His aggressive nature is a reflection of humanity's own.

sources.author: Sir George Grey
sources.description: A classic collection of Māori myths and legends.
sources.text: A classic collection of Māori myths and legends.
